pages.text: and too low silica for producing bricks [2], which could increase shrinkage and bulk density and decrease the strength of bricks, thus results in the bricks could not meet the requirements of the standards. However, the major constituents of the clay and ﬂy ash as the general raw materials for bricks were silicon and aluminum which ac- count for over 75% of the weight. So adding clay and ﬂy ash in proper percentage could make up for the disadvantages of the tailings for producing bricks. Further X-ray diffraction analysis of the tailings and clay used are revealed in Figs. 1 and 2 , which indicated that the major mineral phases of the tailings used were quartz and hematite while calcite and chlorite occurred as minor phases, in the clay quartz, montmorillonite and illite were the major phases and hematite was the minor phase. 2.2. Experimental methods The mixtures with different proportions of hematite tailings, ﬂy ash and clay were ﬁrstly mixed for 10 min in a blender, some water was then added to the mix- tures to homogenize for 10 min. The moistened granules were pressed into cylinder with dimension of U50 /C250 mm for producing brick specimens using a hydraulic press. The formed specimens were dried in an oven at 105 /C176C for 6–8 h, then the dried specimens were ﬁred in an electric furnace. The furnace temperature was gradually increased at a heating rate of 6 /C176C/min from room temperature to desired ﬁring temperature and holding for 2 h, and ﬁnally the ﬁred specimens were cooled in air to room temperature. As required by the Fired Common Bricks Standard (GB/ T5101-2003) [14] for construction bricks, the important properties of bricks includ- ing compressive strength, water absorption and bulk density were tested to deter- mine the appropriate conditions for producing qualiﬁed bricks. The compressive strength of ﬁred specimens was measured by press testing machine (YES-100). Water absorption and bulk density tests were performed in accordance with the methods described in Test Methods for Wall Bricks (GB/ T2542-2003) [15]. The chemical composition of raw materials was determined by X-ray ﬂuorescence (XRF) (AXIOS). The mineral phases of the main raw materials and the ﬁnal ﬁring specimen were identiﬁed with X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D/MX- IIIA). The morphologies of specimens were examined through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Japan, JSM-5610LV). 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Effect of raw materials formulation on the properties of ﬁred specimens The main disadvantage of the hematite tailings in producing bricks is the presence of excessive iron and less silica. On the con- trary, common clay and ﬂy ash usually contain high amounts of silica and aluminum, moreover clay and ﬂy ash often have lower bulk density and are conﬁrmed as good raw materials for making bricks [16,17]. Adding clay and ﬂy ash to the raw materials would increase mechanical strength and reduce bulk density of the bricks. The mixed samples with different proportions of tailings, clay and ﬂy ash were ﬁred at 1000 /C176C which are the general ﬁring tempera- ture of clay bricks manufacturing and holding for 2 h. The proper- ties of ﬁred specimens are given in Table 2 . Results indicated that the bulk density of bricks decreased obviously with the additions of clay and ﬂy ash. However, the compressive strength dropped sharply with the addition of ﬂy ash. When the content of clay was 5%, adding 4% ﬂy ash made the compressive strength dropped to 12.98 MPa. So the

900, 950, 980, 1000, 1030 and 1050 /C176C for 2 h. The inﬂuence of ﬁr- ing temperature on compressive strength and water absorption of the ﬁred specimens is shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3 , the compressive strength values increased and water absorption values reduced with increasing ﬁring tem- perature, especially above 900 /C176C, the increase in compressive strength was more dramatically, it revealed that the temperature of 900 /C176C was the starting ﬁring temperature. But up to 1050 /C176C, the color of specimens became darker and brownish, the ﬁring shrinkage became very obvious, and bulk density of the ﬁred spec- imens were above 2.0 g/cm 3, beyond the range of bricks made with clay which normally have a bulk density of 1.8–2.0 g/cm 3. So the optimum range of ﬁring temperature was 980–1030 /C176C, where mechanical strength and water absorption of the ﬁred specimens were 20.03–22.92 MPa and 16.54–17.93%, respectively, the color of bricks was reddish, the surface of bricks was smooth and free of defects such as cracks or bubbles, the other physical properties and durability were well conformed to Fired Common Bricks Stan- dard (GB/T5101-2003). 3.3. Effect of forming water content on the properties of ﬁred specimens Forming water content is also an important factor affecting the quality of bricks. Too high forming water content will result in Table 2 Effect of different formulation on the properties of ﬁred specimens. higher ﬂowing rate, and even make the bricks deformed. Besides, the evaporation of the excessive water in drying and ﬁring leaves more and larger pores, which would make the mechanical strength decreased and the water absorption increased [21]. But less form- ing water content will bring about the mixtures too incompact to forming. Determined the conditions that the mass ratio of tail- ings:clay:ﬂy ash = 80:10:10, forming pressure was 20 MPa, ﬁring temperature was 1000 /C176C for 2 h, the effect of forming water con- tent on compressive strength and water absorption of bricks is shown in Fig. 4. Results indicated that with forming water content ranged from 5 to 15% the compressive strength increased, but fur- ther increase of forming water content would result in the decrease of compressive strength. Moreover, water absorption values were found to increase with increase in forming water content. So the optimum forming water content was 12.5–15%. 3.4. Effect of forming pressure on the properties of ﬁred specimens Under appropriate pressure, from relax granules to form com- pact unity, the forming process of the mixtures of tailings, clay and ﬂy ash were implemented. Higher forming pressure could lead to more compact touch between granules and more strong cohe- sion and combine force in the bricks, thus results in the higher compressive strength of bricks. Fig. 5 shows the inﬂuence of form- ing pressure on the compressive strength and water absorption of bricks. When the forming pressure varied from 10 to 20 MPa, the compressive strength of brick specimens rapidly increased, but over 20 MPa, the compressive strength valves increased little. The water absorption of brick specimens decreased drastically with increasing forming pressure. Additionally, higher forming pressure results in higher density of bricks, higher energy con- sumption and production cost, so the suitable forming pressure was selected as 20–25 MPa. 3.5. Characterization of ﬁred specimens The phases and morphologies of the ﬁnal ﬁred specimens, which were prepared in the optimum conditions that the mass ra- tio of tailings:clay:ﬂy ash = 84:10:6, forming water content 15%, forming pressure 20 MPa and ﬁring temperature 1000 /C176C for 2 h, were characterized by XRD and SEM. The XRD pattern of the ﬁnal ﬁred specimen is shown in Fig. 6 . Comparing with Fig. 1 revealed that the peaks of calcite and chlorite existing in green tailings dis- appeared, the new peaks of anorthite and tridymite emerged, which indicated that calcite and chlorite in green tailings were decomposed and a small amount of anorthite and tridymite gener- ated in the ﬁring process. The major crystalline phases present in ﬁred specimens were quartz, hematite, anorthite and tridymite, which formed the main mineral framework of ﬁred specimens, were responsible for the mechanical strength of bricks. The microstructure of fractured surface of the dried specimen without ﬁring and the ﬁnal ﬁred specimen are shown in Fig. 7 . The dried specimen revealed compact microstructure and sheet- like appearance of phyllosilicates ( Fig. 7 a), after ﬁred at 1000 /C176C for 2 h, as Fig. 7 b showed clear evidence of vitriﬁcation or partial melting, which was the typical grain and bond microstructure, and crystalline phases were embedded in glassy matrix forming strong entirety, which promoted the strength of bricks. Moreover large amount of interconnected and irregular pores were formed due to water evaporation and dehydroxylatio

Preparation of eco-friendly construction bricks from hematite tailings Yongliang Chen a,⇑, Yimin Zhang a,⇑, Tiejun Chen a, Yunliang Zhao b, Shenxu Bao b a College of Resource and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China b College of Resource and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China article info Article history: Received 30 June 2010 Received in revised form 29 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 16 December 2010 Keywords: Hematite tailings Utilization Bricks Properties Microstructure abstract With the objective of reducing the negative impacts on environment and utilizing the secondary resource of tailings, the possibility of making construction bricks by using the hematite tailings from western Hubei province of China was investigated. Besides hematite tailings, the additives of clay and ﬂy ash were added to the raw materials to improve the brick quality. Through the process of mixing, forming, drying and ﬁring, the bricks were produced. The optimum conditions were found to be that the hematite tailings content were as high as 84%, forming water content and forming pressure were respectively in the range of 12.5–15% and 20–25 MPa, and the suitable ﬁring temperature was ranged from 980 to 1030 /C176C for 2 h. Under these conditions, the mechanical strength and water absorption of the reddish ﬁred specimens were 20.03–22.92 MPa and 16.54–17.93%, respectively, and the other physical properties and durability were well conformed to Chinese Fired Common Bricks Standard (GB/T5101-2003). The phases and mor- phologies of the green tailings and ﬁred specimen were characterized by XRD and SEM. The results showed that the main mineral phases of the product were hematite, quartz, anorthite and tridymite, which were principally responsible for the mechanical strength of bricks. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Iron tailings as a kind of waste products after ore dressing is one of the major industrial solid wastes. The rapid development of iron and steel industry has led to the increasing amount of iron tailings. According to statistic, there are more than 150 kinds of mineral re- sources in over 8000 operating mines in China, which have dis- carded and stacked about 5.97 billion tons of tailings till now, the iron tailings stacked account for about one third of the total tailings, but there are still continuously discharged and stacked over 300 million tons per year [1,2]. Few reuse and large stockpile of iron tailings have led to a number of environmental and social problems [3–5] . In recent years, comprehensive utilization of tail- ings has been receiving considerable attention, especially recovery of iron and other metals from tailings [6–12] , which is a well- recognized process because of substantial economical beneﬁts. However, it generates the problems of disposal and secondary pol- lution again. However, using tailings to produce building materials not only can realize zero-emission of tailings wastes, but also would offer a new raw material for building industry, which is a more effective resource recovery alternative. On the other hand, for protecting clay resource and developing eco-friendly building materials, the Chinese government has for- bidden producing and using normal clay solid bricks in many cities, and has been actively advocating the use of solid wastes for mak- ing building materials to
